caption.text: Here, I’ll give you one UX shortcut that reduces
dropout in your onboarding by a lot.
The emotional progress bar.
Most SaaS have a progress bar.
Step 1/4, step 2/4. It’s informative, it’s useful, but it’s cold and mechanical,
we don’t really know what the steps are for.
Imagine in a HR SaaS,
a recruiter is creating their account, filling in their company info,
setting up their space and they see at the top:
“Step 2/4” but in the end,
they don’t really know why they’re following these steps or what happens next.
Now
imagine the same scenario, but at each step they see a different message.
Step 1: we’re starting, in a few minutes
your offer will be visible to hundreds of candidates.
Step 2: well done,
just 2 steps left and your candidates can picture themselves in your company.
Step 3: we’re almost there, just one step before publishing your offer. Step 4:
this is it, publish and watch the applications roll in.
This second scenario
completely changes the game. Because you’re no longer telling them where they are in a process,
you’re telling them where they are in their goal.
And because you’re creating an emotional progression.
At each step,
they feel something different,
the excitement of getting started,
the concrete progress,
the anticipation and the sense of achievement.
A user who knows where they’re going keeps going. In the end, the emotional progress bar
is easy to set up. At each step of your onboarding,
ask yourself just one question:
“What is my user feeling here,
and above all, what will make them want to go to the next step?”
Result: your user no longer just goes through your onboarding,
they move forward toward something they really want to reach.
